_meta.notes: Communication modes per race. Used by the narrator (regionFlavor block) and the dialogue system to render in-character utterance modes — orcs write rather than speak; halflings use signal flags at sea; dwarves use kite-signals across the cloud-mass; humans speak directly; elves speak directly with the act. Mechanical implications: certain agreements (oaths, contracts) require the right modality.

human.summary: direct speech, time-pressured. apprentices whisper; masters speak slowly.
human.narratorHint: render speech as direct quotation. masters use shorter sentences than apprentices.

elven.summary: spoken with the act in public; off-act in private. ledger-mind tracks favors.
elven.narratorHint: in public: breathy, rhythmic, performative. off-act: clipped, profane. switch when context shifts.

dwarven.summary: wind-paced spoken communication; Skybeam pollination kites carry coded signals across cloud-mass.
dwarven.narratorHint: long pauses between sentences. cross-terrace messages render as kite-signal observations.

kiteSignal.summary: single-tone bone-flute attached to a pollination kite; specific frequencies correspond to specific messages.
examples: two short, one long — emergency at lower terrace

halfling.summary: spoken at close range; waxed signal flags between ships and from reef-edge platforms.
halfling.narratorHint: spoken halfling is clipped and salt-cured. signal flags are in named patterns (flagcloth via 'drowncaller-flag-cloth').

signalFlag.summary: heavy waxed canvas in halfling colors. specific pattern combinations are legible at sea-distance.
examples: single-cross — wreck imminent, do not approach

orcish.summary: writing is the default; quiet spoken speech happens but the orc is faintly disappointed in themselves for it.
orcish.narratorHint: render orcish 'speech' as marginalia on a passed folio. Hush-readers communicate ONLY in marginalia.

marginalia.summary: single careful word in the margin of a passed book; the receiver writes their reply nearby.

mixed-metropolis.summary: Caelum's working pidgin lets all five races trade in cargo zones. No fluent native speakers; everyone uses it.
mixed-metropolis.narratorHint: render pidgin transactions as short clipped exchanges with switching scripts.

mechanicalImplications.summary: An oath given verbally to an orc is not binding by their custom. The oath must be written down + countersigned in marginalia.
mechanicalImplications.narratorEffect: When a player promises something verbally to an orcish NPC, the NPC will hand them paper without comment. Refusing to write seals nothing.

mechanicalImplications.summary: Hiring the halfling navy requires the customer to fly a recognized flag. No flag, no contract.
mechanicalImplications.narratorEffect: A flagless ship at the Coral Anchorage will be refused service politely. Buying flagcloth at the Anchorage is the customary first step.

mechanicalImplications.summary: Crossing onto a Highfield orchard terrace requires Gust-watcher clearance, signaled by kite. Without clearance, the path is closed by old custom.
mechanicalImplications.narratorEffect: Trespassers are not assaulted; they are simply not addressed by anyone, until a Gust-watcher's kite is posted.

mechanicalImplications.summary: Branchmen offer better terms to clients who endure the act for three full meetings. Customers who skip the act pay 10-15% more.
mechanicalImplications.narratorEffect: The narrator can describe an explicit 'act discount' in pricing dialogue.

mechanicalImplications.summary: A human master's last commission cannot be refused by the apprentice. Even if the apprentice is in another city, the request reaches them and is honored.
mechanicalImplications.narratorEffect: If a Threadwarden master commissions a piece in their last year, the prose treats the apprentice's response as inevitable.
